thread.title: Quebec's new delegate general in NY talks North Country partnership in visit
thread.title_full: Quebec's new delegate general in NY talks North Country partnership in visit

author: CARLY NEWTON Press-Republican
title: Quebec's new delegate general in NY talks North Country partnership in visit
text:
PLATTSBURGH — In his role as Quebec’s new delegate general in New York, David Brulotte aims to reinforce the strong partnership between Canada and the United States as much as possible. The North Country could be key in doing that.
“The U.S. is our main partner for everything and anything: trade, investment, border security, continental security, public affairs, government affairs,” Brulotte said in a sit-down interview with the Press-Republican Tuesday.
Brulotte, who previously served as the delegate general in Los Angeles and before that in Tokyo, was appointed to his new position in New York in August. His “mandate” is to promote trade and investment into Quebec across the state, Delaware, Kentucky, Maryland, New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania, Virginia and West Virginia.
In the North Country, however, he recognized his goal will be easier to accomplish.
Brulotte said when he made his first visit here two weeks ago, he didn’t have to introduce Quebec like he normally would have.
“It’s always an interesting pitch to sell Quebec, or to introduce Quebec to wherever you are on the globe,” he said.
“Here is completely different, because anyone could do my pitch, because people are very used to Quebec, not only geographically, but because of the trade, the tourism and the intertwined communities. So it’s been fascinating, because the impact that both regions have on one another is extremely significant.”
CHPE
The Champlain-Hudson Power Express, or “CHPE,” a major cross-border energy project on the North Country’s notable Lake Champlain is one example of the cross-border impact.
The project involves the construction of an underground and underwater transmission line spanning approximately 339 miles between the Canada–U.S. border and New York City.
CHPE, expected to go online in 2026, will provide competitively priced hydropower from Québec that is expected to lower climate emissions and local air pollutants, as well as electricity generation costs throughout the state by $17 billion over the first 25 years of operation, all the while providing increased reliability and resiliency for the downstate grid, project officials previously said.
“So that’s a big sector for us — energy — and not only energy from Quebec, but also the decarbonization of the Northeastern U.S. and Canada,” Brulotte said.
“The power line will not only provide hydroelectricity to New York, but it’s a bi-directional installation. So down the road if, at some point … Quebec and New York produces, let’s say, wind power, or other types of energy, the CHPE will be able to sort of provide a more secure grid over the northeast.”
BATTERIES
Beyond CHPE, Brulotte said Quebec, home to critical minerals like lithium and cobalt, hopes to eventually be a major battery manufacturer for New York and the northeast as the era of clean energy continues to expand.
“We have very similar goals … both Quebec, New York and other states, either in the Northeast or elsewhere in the U.S., have goals of decarbonization on their own by 2035 or 2040, depending on the sectors of transit,” he said.
“Quebec has the highest EV (electric vehicle) adoption rate in Canada. We have the largest fleet of individual EV in Canada, per capita. So we’re already very focused on that sector.”
He said it’s a perfect opportunity for the two countries and especially New York to work together and be less dependent on other nations for supplies.
“At some point down the line, 10 years-ish, a good percentage of battery components within North America will actually be supplied by Quebec, and that’s a goal. I think that got fast tracked over the last couple of years, because the U.S. and Canada want to make sure that our supply chains are more resilient, more integrated together, and not as dependent on other nations as we used to be,” Brulotte said.
“So I see great potential in this. Quebec has put a lot of energy and efforts on this, but so has Canada and Ontario … it’s a concerted effort on that front.”
TRANSPORTATION
When it comes to the transportation sector between the North Country and Quebec, Brulotte said it’s another area that is strong and constantly improving.
For example, the manufacturing company Alstom, which has a facility in the City of Plattsburgh and produces rail cars, is a Quebec client. Brulotte also pointed to the mobility week that is held annually in Plattsburgh and allows for Quebec and American companies to meet and network with each other.
This event gives Canadian businesses, especially those looking to potentially expand into the U.S., a chance to learn about Plattsburgh and the broader region.
“I think the region is a fantastic opportunity for our businesses who want to expand in the U.S., not only because of the geography — you can go back on the weekends, to your family, to Montreal or other regions — but because there’s already several Quebecers here, the welcoming community is very accustomed to Quebec,” he said.
“The ties are strong … I think it’s a nice opportunity that we have, collectively, including my team at the New York office, to just promote more the border region here in the North Country.”
OPPORTUNITY
And while the relationship between Quebec and the North Country is already well established, Brulotte said he sees room for more opportunities and growth, which he hopes to help pioneer in his 3-5 year tenure.
“The thing I will work on is to put much more emphasis and have the region be in the limelight for us in Quebec, on the business, on the trade aspect, on the shared sort of values and families,” he said.
“A lot of Quebecers know of the North Country. They know of Plattsburgh, obviously, most Quebecers who traveled to the U.S. will have driven through Plattsburgh probably once in their lives, if not, several times. But that’s kind of it for a lot of people, it’s more of a transit city or region of New York,” he continued.
“I think very few people in Quebec would know of this apart if you have business here, so I think that creates a huge opportunity for us to sort of showcase and highlight what the region has to offer.”
